explanation: The natural language statement "If P, then Q" translates to the LTL implication "P -> Q". The phrase "shall be" indicates that this condition must hold globally, at all times. Therefore, the LTL formula G(not_Eclipse_equalTo_1 -> sun_angle_lessThan_45) accurately captures that whenever 'not_Eclipse_equalTo_1' is true, 'sun_angle_lessThan_45' must also be true at that exact same time, and this must hold throughout the system's execution.

The natural language statement "If A, then B" expresses a conditional relationship that must hold at all times. The phrase "shall be" reinforces this global requirement. Therefore, the LTL formula G(not_Eclipse_equalTo_1 -> sun_angle_lessThan_45) accurately captures that globally, if 'not_Eclipse_equalTo_1' is true, then 'sun_angle_lessThan_45' must also be true at that same instant.

The statement "If A, then B" implies a global implication: whenever A occurs, B must also occur. The 'G' (Globally) operator ensures that this implication holds true at all points in time. Therefore, G(not_Eclipse_equalTo_1 -> sun_angle_lessThan_45) correctly captures that if 'not_Eclipse_equalTo_1' is true at any point, then 'sun_angle_lessThan_45' must also be true at that same point.

The natural language statement describes a global invariant: if the condition 'not_Eclipse is equal to 1' holds, then 'sun_angle is less than 45' must also hold simultaneously. The LTL formula G(A -> B) accurately captures this 'always implies' relationship, meaning that at every point in time, if A is true, then B must also be true at that same point.

The statement "If A, then B" translates to the LTL implication "A -> B". The phrase "shall be" implies that this condition must hold at all times, which is captured by the Global operator (G). Therefore, the LTL property G(not_Eclipse_equalTo_1 -> sun_angle_lessThan_45) correctly expresses that whenever not_Eclipse is equal to 1, it must always be the case that sun_angle is less than 45.

The natural language statement describes a continuous implication: whenever 'not_Eclipse_equalTo_1' is true, 'sun_angle_lessThan_45' must also be true. The word "If... then... shall be" indicates that this condition must hold at all times. Therefore, the 'G' (Globally) operator is used to assert that the implication 'not_Eclipse_equalTo_1 -> sun_angle_lessThan_45' is always true.

The statement describes a global implication: if 'not_Eclipse_equalTo_1' is true at any point in time, then 'sun_angle_lessThan_45' must also be true at that same point in time. The 'If...then' structure indicates an implication, and 'shall be' implies that this condition must always hold true, which is captured by the global operator (G).

The natural language statement describes a safety property: if 'not_Eclipse_equalTo_1' is true at any point in time, then 'sun_angle_lessThan_45' must also be true at that exact same point in time. The 'If A, then B' structure translates to a logical implication (A -> B). The phrase 'shall be' implies that this condition must hold at all times, which is captured by the Global (G) operator.

The statement "If A, then B" translates directly to A -> B. The phrase "shall be" indicates that this condition must hold true at all times, implying a global property. Therefore, the G (globally) operator is applied to the implication. 'not_Eclipse_equalTo_1' represents 'the value of signal not_Eclipse is equal to 1', and 'sun_angle_lessThan_45' represents 'the value of signal sun_angle shall be less than 45'. Thus, the LTL 'G(not_Eclipse_equalTo_1 -> sun_angle_lessThan_45)' means that globally, if not_Eclipse is 1, then sun_angle must be less than 45.

The statement "If the value of signal not_Eclipse is equal to 1, then the value of signal sun_angle shall be less than 45" describes a continuous implication. The phrase "If A, then B" combined with "shall be" indicates that whenever the condition A (not_Eclipse_equalTo_1) is true, then condition B (sun_angle_lessThan_45) must also be true at that same time, and this rule must hold globally (at all times). Therefore, a global operator (G) is used to wrap the implication (->) between the two atomic propositions.
